steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an Inferences question. The passage presents a logical argument: (1) Scottish English strongly emphasizes the “r” sound, (2) Upland South dialects show the same emphasis, (3) the Upland South was colonized largely by Scottish-descended people. The word “Thus” signals that the blank must state the conclusion that logically follows from these three facts.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the English dialects spoken in the Upland South acquired their emphasis on the “r” sound from dialects spoken in Scotland.': This is the conclusion that follows directly from the evidence. The colonizers came from Scotland and brought their dialect features; those features are now present in the Upland South. Direction of influence: Scotland → Upland South. ✓
  • Choice B — 'emphasis on the “r” sound will eventually spread from English dialects spoken in the Upland South to dialects spoken elsewhere.': Nothing in the passage suggests future spread to other regions. This goes beyond what the evidence supports. ✗
  • Choice C — 'the English dialects spoken in Scotland were influenced by dialects spoken in the Upland South.': This reverses the direction of influence. The Upland South was colonized by Scottish-descended people, not the other way around. Scotland cannot have been influenced by a region that didn't exist until after Scotland was settled. ✗
  • Choice D — 'people from Scotland abandoned their emphasis on the “r” sound after relocating to the Upland South.': This contradicts the evidence. The Upland South dialects still show strong “r” emphasis, meaning the feature was preserved, not abandoned. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
